paper_title: Strengthening Mechanisms in Low Density Fe–26Mn–xAl–1C Steels
citation_sentences: In our previous study, it was indicated that steels containing 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al exhibited moderate yield strength of 321, 378, and 470 MPa, respectively, whereas 12Al steel indicated yield strength of 952 MPa.[5] a–c shows that 3Al, 6Al, Figure 1and 10Al steels were fully austenitic when imaged using an optical microscope, while ferrite phase was observed in 12Al steel, Figure 1d. Excluding the twin boundaries, the grain size of 3Al, 6Al, and 10Al steels was similar at 80 μm, whereas the grain size of austenite in 12Al steel was significantly smaller ( 40 μm). When the twin boundaries were considered, the grain size of 10Al steel was 39 μm and that of 12Al steel was 16 μm. The volume fraction of ferrite (small white block phase in Figure 1d) measured by image analysis technique was 7%.

TEM observations indicated only fundamental reflections in 3Al and 6Al steels, but weak superlattice spots between the fundamental reflections appeared in 10Al steel (Figure 1e) and strong ones in 12Al steel (Figure 1f), indicating the presence of $( \mathrm { F e , M n } ) _ { 3 } \mathrm { A l C _ { x } }$ carbides (ordered κ carbides).

It was stated that κ-carbides can precipitate even during rapid cooling from the solution treatment temperature provided that the driving force for precipitation is adequate.[15] Increasing Al-content can increase the driving force for the precipitation of κ phase.[16] It was pointed out that κ-carbides can form during quenching if Al- content exceeds 10 wt% and the alloy contains adequate C-content,[17] which was observed in our case. It was observed that the two sets of modulation appeared in the as-quenched 12Al steel (Figure 1f) and that the modulation image is the strain contrast that originates from lattice modulation, and is caused by fluctuation in the concentration of solute atoms, especially interstitial carbon atoms, and to a lesser extent by large substitutional aluminum atoms.[10] Taking an analogy of the present case to aging in Fe–Mn–Al–C steels, it appears that the increase in yieldstrength of 361.93 MPa is causedby κ carbides in the as-quenched 12Al steel.

citation_sentences: To evaluate the influence of grain size, different heat treatment schedules were adopted in 6Al steel. The engineering stress–strain plots of steels are presented in Figure 2a. A range of grain size (5.38–52 μm) with twin boundaries in 6Al steels were obtained and the Hall–Petch relationship (Eq. 2) between yield strength and grain size is presented in b.
